content: Ruiz et al. Simulation Study of Airport Security Screening Checkpoints (PMC12043019). Extended an existing SIMIO-based DES model to represent a single checkpoint at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport. Model scope: single checkpoint, full day of operations, interarrival times discretized into 15-minute periods. Entities: Passengers (regular and PreCheck), Carry-on baggage, Checked baggage. Resources: Travel Document Checkers (TDCs), Transportation Security Officers (TSOs), X-ray inspection machines, Advanced Imaging Technology (AIT) body scanners, Walk-through metal detectors (WTMDs), Conveyor belts, Screening lanes. Activities: document verification at TDC -> item placement on conveyor -> X-ray inspection -> AIT body scan -> optional secondary inspection -> exit. Metrics: average passenger cycle time, throughput, TDC utilization, queue lengths. Scenarios tested in the paper: benchmark proportional allocation, best-case fully staffed, simulation-optimization dynamic allocation, reactive staffing (add staff when cycle time > 20 min), proactive staffing (add staff when predicted cycle time > 10 min). Simulation technology: SIMIO with OptQuest optimizer.
provenance_note: Academic paper directly describing a SIMIO DES model of airport security at Phoenix Sky Harbor. Open-access via PMC; clean fetch, no friction.
labels: pmc, simio, phoenix sky harbor, full day, ruiz-et-al

content: GitHub repository aschatz1995/Airport-Security-Wait-Sim. README describes the project as a SimPy simulation of airport security checkpoint operations, focused on finding the optimal number of ID check queues and baggage scanners to keep wait times under a threshold. The README itself is a one-line description. The real model parameters and scenarios live inside a Jupyter notebook in the repo which is not accessible via automated HTML fetch.
provenance_note: GitHub README is thin; the substantive content lives in a Jupyter notebook. Represents one node in a teaching-assignment cluster (see context row 3).

content: Canonical Georgia Tech ISYE 6501 airport security homework parameters, aggregated from multiple search-engine result snippets rather than a single source fetch. Arrivals: Poisson process with lambda = 5 passengers per minute. ID check service: exponential distribution with mu = 0.75 minutes. Personal scanner: Uniform(0.5, 1.0) minutes. Several servers with shortest-queue routing for scanner assignment. Typical use: find the minimal count of ID check queues and scanners such that average passenger wait time stays under a specified threshold. This assignment has produced a widely-implemented teaching-assignment cluster: known SimPy implementations on GitHub include aschatz1995, pkusunbx, slimhindrance, olivierzach, and adpoe. Per the Seeder's teaching-assignment-cluster deduplication pattern, these are treated as a single canonical model rather than N parallel blueprints.
provenance_note: Aggregated from multiple search result snippets rather than fetched from any single URL. Represents the canonical Georgia Tech ISYE 6501 homework assignment that spawned a cluster of independent SimPy student implementations. Labeled second-party because the content was accumulated from search-engine summaries of individual sources. Retroactively updated to use the search_snippet_aggregation acquisition method (added to the schema to handle exactly this case).
